front: What is the primary purpose of the SIGCLD signal in Unix?
back: To notify a parent process when the status of one of its child processes has changed, typically meaning the child has terminated or stopped.

front: What is a key historical behavioral problem with SIGCLD in System V Unix?
back: The signal was unreliable by default. If the signal was set to the default action (SIG_DFL) or ignored (SIG_IGN), zombie processes would not be reaped. Furthermore, the signal would reset to SIG_DFL after being caught, requiring re-installation of the handler within the handler itself.

front: How does the behavior of SIGCHLD differ from the traditional SIGCLD signal?
back: SIGCHLD is the POSIX-standard name for the signal and has more reliable semantics. It is the preferred name to use in modern programs. When explicitly set to SIG_IGN, the system automatically reaps child processes, preventing them from becoming zombies.

front: What is the correct way to wait for all children and avoid zombies using the signal handler?
back: The signal handler should call one of the wait functions (e.g., `wait()` or `waitpid()`) with the WNOHANG option in a loop to reap all terminated children until no more remain, preventing the handler from blocking.

front: What crucial function must be called within a SIGCHLD handler to avoid missing subsequent signals on some systems?
back: The signal() function must be called to re-establish the handler, as some older Unix variants (e.g., System V) reset the signal action to default (SIG_DFL) when the handler is invoked.
